title: Exercises: Creation and Annihilation Operators
description: Practice working with creation and annihilation operators

exercises.title: Bosonic Operator Matrices
exercises.description: Construct matrix representations of bosonic operators.
exercises.instructions: Implement functions to create matrix representations of bosonic creation and annihilation operators for a truncated Fock space.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np

def bosonic_annihilation(n_max):
    """Create annihilation operator matrix.
    
    Args:
        n_max: Maximum occupation number (matrix size)
    
    Returns:
        n_max × n_max matrix for operator a
    """
    # Your code here
    # a|n⟩ = √n |n-1⟩
    pass

def bosonic_creation(n_max):
    """Create creation operator matrix.
    
    Args:
        n_max: Maximum occupation number (matrix size)
    
    Returns:
        n_max × n_max matrix for operator a†
    """
    # Your code here
    # Hint: a† = (a)ᵀ (transpose, since real)
    pass

def verify_commutation(a, a_dag):
    """Verify [a, a†] = 1."""
    # Your code here
    pass

# Test
n_max = 5
a = bosonic_annihilation(n_max)
a_dag = bosonic_creation(n_max)

print("Annihilation operator:")
print(a)
print("\nCreation operator:")
print(a_dag)
print("\nCommutator [a, a†]:")
print(verify_commutation(a, a_dag))
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np

def bosonic_annihilation(n_max):
    """Create annihilation operator matrix."""
    a = np.zeros((n_max, n_max))
    for n in range(1, n_max):
        a[n-1, n] = np.sqrt(n)
    return a

def bosonic_creation(n_max):
    """Create creation operator matrix."""
    return bosonic_annihilation(n_max).T

def verify_commutation(a, a_dag):
    """Verify [a, a†] = 1."""
    commutator = a @ a_dag - a_dag @ a
    return commutator

# Test
n_max = 5
a = bosonic_annihilation(n_max)
a_dag = bosonic_creation(n_max)

print("Annihilation operator:")
print(a)
print("\nCreation operator:")
print(a_dag)
print("\nCommutator [a, a†]:")
comm = verify_commutation(a, a_dag)
print(comm)
print(f"\nIs identity (except corner)? {np.allclose(comm[:-1,:-1], np.eye(n_max-1))}")
hints: a|n⟩ = √n |n-1⟩, so matrix element a[n-1, n] = √n
Creation is the transpose of annihilation for real matrices

exercises.title: Fermionic Operators

    Returns:
        2×2 matrix for c†

    """Verify {c, c†} = 1."""

    pass

    """Verify (c†)² = 0."""

# Test

c_dag = fermionic_creation()

print("c =")
print(c)
print("\nc† =")
print(c_dag)

print(verify_anticommutation(c, c_dag))
print("\n(c†)² =")
print(verify_pauli_exclusion(c_dag))
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np

def fermionic_annihilation():
    """Create single-mode fermionic annihilation operator."""
    # c|0⟩ = 0, c|1⟩ = |0⟩
    return np.array([[0, 1],
                     [0, 0]])

def fermionic_creation():
    """Create single-mode fermionic creation operator."""
    # c†|0⟩ = |1⟩, c†|1⟩ = 0
    return np.array([[0, 0],
                     [1, 0]])

def verify_anticommutation(c, c_dag):
    """Verify {c, c†} = 1."""
    return c @ c_dag + c_dag @ c

def verify_pauli_exclusion(c_dag):
    """Verify (c†)² = 0."""
    return c_dag @ c_dag

# Test
c = fermionic_annihilation()
c_dag = fermionic_creation()

print("c =")
print(c)
print("\nc† =")
print(c_dag)
print("\n{c, c†} =")
print(verify_anticommutation(c, c_dag))
print("\n(c†)² =")
print(verify_pauli_exclusion(c_dag))
hints: For fermions, basis is just |0⟩ and |1⟩

exercises.title: State Building
exercises.description: Calculate the result of applying creation operators.
exercises.instructions:
For bosonic operators, calculate:

a) a†|0⟩
b) a†a†|0⟩ (normalized)
c) a|2⟩
d) a†a|3⟩
e) aa†|2⟩

Express each result as a ket with proper normalization.

exercises.title: Two-Mode Fermionic System
exercises.description: Build operators for a two-orbital fermionic system.
exercises.instructions: Using the Jordan-Wigner transformation, construct creation and annihilation operators for two fermionic modes.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np

def pauli_matrices():
    """Return Pauli matrices and identity."""
    I = np.eye(2)
    X = np.array([[0, 1], [1, 0]])
    Y = np.array([[0, -1j], [1j, 0]])
    Z = np.array([[1, 0], [0, -1]])
    return I, X, Y, Z

def sigma_minus():
    """Return σ⁻ = (X + iY)/2."""
    # Your code here
    pass

def sigma_plus():
    """Return σ⁺ = (X - iY)/2."""
    # Your code here
    pass

def two_mode_operators():
    """Build c₁, c₁†, c₂, c₂† for two fermionic modes.
    
    Jordan-Wigner transformation:
    c₁ = σ⁻ ⊗ I
    c₂ = Z ⊗ σ⁻
    
    Returns:
        c1, c1_dag, c2, c2_dag as 4×4 matrices
    """
    # Your code here
    pass

# Test
c1, c1_dag, c2, c2_dag = two_mode_operators()

# Verify anticommutation
print("{c₁, c₁†} =")
print(c1 @ c1_dag + c1_dag @ c1)
print("\n{c₂, c₂†} =")
print(c2 @ c2_dag + c2_dag @ c2)
print("\n{c₁, c₂†} =")
print(c1 @ c2_dag + c2_dag @ c1)
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np

def pauli_matrices():
    """Return Pauli matrices and identity."""
    I = np.eye(2)
    X = np.array([[0, 1], [1, 0]])
    Y = np.array([[0, -1j], [1j, 0]])
    Z = np.array([[1, 0], [0, -1]])
    return I, X, Y, Z

def sigma_minus():
    """Return σ⁻ = (X + iY)/2."""
    I, X, Y, Z = pauli_matrices()
    return (X + 1j*Y) / 2

def sigma_plus():
    """Return σ⁺ = (X - iY)/2."""
    I, X, Y, Z = pauli_matrices()
    return (X - 1j*Y) / 2

def two_mode_operators():
    """Build c₁, c₁†, c₂, c₂† for two fermionic modes."""
    I, X, Y, Z = pauli_matrices()
    sm = sigma_minus()
    sp = sigma_plus()
    
    # Jordan-Wigner transformation
    c1 = np.kron(sm, I)
    c1_dag = np.kron(sp, I)
    c2 = np.kron(Z, sm)
    c2_dag = np.kron(Z, sp)
    
    return c1, c1_dag, c2, c2_dag

# Test
c1, c1_dag, c2, c2_dag = two_mode_operators()

print("{c₁, c₁†} =")
print(np.real(c1 @ c1_dag + c1_dag @ c1))
print("\n{c₂, c₂†} =")
print(np.real(c2 @ c2_dag + c2_dag @ c2))
print("\n{c₁, c₂†} =")
print(np.real(c1 @ c2_dag + c2_dag @ c1))
print("\nAll anticommutation relations satisfied!")
hints: σ⁻ = (X + iY)/2 is the lowering operator
The Z in c₂ = Z ⊗ σ⁻ is the Jordan-Wigner string

exercises.title: Bosonic Number States
exercises.description: Prove the normalization of bosonic number states.
exercises.instructions: Show that |n⟩ = (a†)ⁿ|0⟩/√(n!) is properly normalized, i.e., ⟨n|n⟩ = 1.

proof: Step 2: Use [a, a†] = 1 to normal order
We need to move all a's to the right of all a†'s.
Since a|0⟩ = 0, only the 'n!' term survives:
solution.answer: Using the commutation relation [a, a†] = 1 and a|0⟩ = 0, we find (a)ⁿ(a†)ⁿ|0⟩ = n!|0⟩, which gives ⟨n|n⟩ = (1/n!) × n! = 1.

hints: Use [a, a†] = 1 to derive a(a†)ⁿ = (a†)ⁿa + n(a†)ⁿ⁻¹
